preparation_method: typically served with soy sauce and ginger

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of steamed Soon Hock is approximately 100g of fish flesh, often presented as a whole small fish or a portion of a larger fish served with light soy sauce, ginger, and spring onion garnish.

nutrient_source.title: Oxyeleotris marmorata, raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / FAO/INFOODS SEA Fish Composition Data; adapted for steamed preparation using standard moisture-loss factor
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for Marble Goby (Oxyeleotris marmorata) exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central by species name. Values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy generic freshwater fish (perch/bass family, lean white-fleshed fish) and cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA regional fish composition data for comparable lean freshwater species. Steaming causes minimal macronutrient change relative to raw; values represent steamed edible portion per 100g. Sodium reflects light soy sauce dressing typical of restaurant preparation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of protein-dominant fish foods — University of Sydney GI Database; pure protein/fat foods convention
gi_source.source_match_notes: Marble Goby is a lean white-fleshed freshwater fish with negligible carbohydrate content (0g per 100g). Pure protein and fat foods do not elicit a meaningful glycemic response. The University of Sydney GI Database confirms that fish and seafood products with zero carbohydrate content have an effective GI of zero. Per Veritas convention for zero-carbohydrate foods, glycemic_index is set to 1 (lowest valid positive sentinel), gi_category is 'low', and glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0.

hypertension.guidance: Steamed Marble Goby is a lean, low-fat protein source, but the typical restaurant preparation includes light soy sauce which contributes approximately 280mg sodium per 100g; individuals with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce or consume smaller portions to manage sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.guidance: Steamed Marble Goby contains effectively zero carbohydrates and has a glycemic index of 1, making it an excellent high-protein, low-glycemic food choice for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes; it supports blood glucose stability when consumed as part of a balanced meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Lean protein with low saturated fat and beneficial omega-3 fatty acids
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Marble Goby is a lean freshwater fish with low saturated fat (0.4g/100g) and a favourable polyunsaturated-to-saturated fat ratio; regular consumption of lean fish is associated with reduced cardiovascular risk, though sodium from soy sauce preparation should be monitored in patients with concurrent hypertension.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Soon Hock, known in English as Marble Goby (scientific name: Oxyeleotris marmorata), is a highly prized freshwater fish in Singapore and Southeast Asian Chinese cuisine. It is renowned for its exceptionally sweet, delicate, and tender white flesh with minimal bones, making it one of the most sought-after and premium-priced fish in Chinese seafood restaurants. The fish is most commonly prepared by steaming whole or in portions, served with a light dressing of soy sauce, sesame oil, ginger slices, and spring onion — a preparation that preserves its natural sweetness and delicate texture. Soon Hock is a lean, high-protein fish with approximately 19.4g protein and only 1.8g fat per 100g, providing around 96 kcal per 100g. Its negligible carbohydrate content makes it suitable for low-glycemic and diabetic-friendly diets. The fish is rich in B vitamins (particularly B12 and niacin), selenium, phosphorus, and potassium. In Singapore, it is a staple of zi char (Chinese home-style cooking) restaurants and upscale seafood establishments. The sodium content is primarily attributable to the soy sauce dressing used in restaurant preparation. Clinically, it is an excellent protein source for patients managing Type 2 Diabetes, cardiovascular disease, or those requiring high-quality lean protein with low saturated fat.
common_user_queries: Is Soon Hock good for diabetics?
What does Marble Goby taste like?
Soon Hock vs other fish nutrition
Is Soon Hock high in protein?

confidence_reason: No species-specific entry for Oxyeleotris marmorata (Marble Goby) exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy lean freshwater fish data and cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA regional fish composition data for comparable species. The adaptation is scientifically defensible given the fish's known lean white-flesh profile, but direct species-level measurement data from an approved primary source was not available. Confidence is therefore medium.
